coredata.dc:title: Red fluorescent carbon dots for sensitive and selective detection and reduction of Au3+
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Xiangcao
Hu, Qian
Yang, Ke
Zhao, Shaojing
Zhu, Shaohua
Wang, Benhua
Zhang, Yi
Yi, Jianing
Song, Xiangzhi
Lan, Minhuan
coredata.dc:description: Simultaneous detection and reduction of Au3+ in contaminated water is extremely desirable. Herein, we synthesized red fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) for Au3+ detection with dual colorimetric and fluorescent spectral response within 2 min. The CDs showed high sensitivity and selectivity towards Au3+ with a low detection limit of 29 nM. Further, CDs selectively reduce Au3+ to Au0 via redox reaction between the thioether group on the surface of CDs and Au3+. Accordingly, CDs were effectively utilized to detect and extract Au3+ without interference from other metal ions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescent probe
Colorimetric
Gold recovery
Waste water

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence determination of Au3+ in aqueous solution
Optimizing conditions and fluorescent detection of Au3+
Specificity of CDs toward Au3+ in analysis of real samples
The sensing mechanism of CDs and reduction of Au3+ in wastewater

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of CDs. (a) TEM and HRTEM images, (b) AFM image, (c) Raman, and (d) FTIR spectrum.

Optical properties of CDs. (a) UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of CDs. (b) Fluorescence spectra of CDs (0.48 μg/mL) obtained under different excitation wavelengths. Time-dependent normalized (c) absorbance of CDs and fluorescein sodium at 468 and 480 nm, respectively, and (d) fluorescence intensity of spectra of CDs and fluorescein sodium at 637 and 514 nm, respectively, under xenon lamp irradiation (1 W/cm2 for 60 min).

(a) The fluorescent intensity of the CDs at 637 nm at different pH values. The insets are the photographs of CDs aqueous solutions at different pH conditions. (b) Time-dependent fluorescence intensity of CDs at 637 nm in the presence of 6 μM Au3+ at different pH levels. (c) The UV-Vis absorption spectra of CDs, Au3+ and CDs+Au3+ in solutions at pH 7. The insets are the photographs of CDs (left) and CDs+Au3+ (right). (d) Fluorescence spectra of CDs in the presence of different concentrations of Au3+. The insets are the fluorescent images of CDs (left) and CDs +Au3+ (right) aqueous solutions under illumination with a green laser. All fluorescence spectra were excited at 520 nm.

(a) Fluorescence spectra and (b) fluorescence intensity of CDs (0.48 μg/mL) in the presence of interfering ions, reaction time = 2 min and pH = 7. The interfering ions include: Ag+, Al3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Eu3+, Fe2+, Fe3+; Gd3+, Hg2+, K+, Mn2+, Na+, NH4 +, Ni2+, and Pb2+ (8.0 μM).

Analysis of sensing and reduction mechanisms. (a) TEM and (b) AFM images of CDs+Au3+; (c) Fluorescence lifetime spectra of CDs (red dots) and CDs+Au3+ (blue dots); (d) FT-IR spectra of CDs, and CDs+Au3+; (e) XPS survey spectrum of CDs+Au3+; (f) High-resolution XPS spectra of Au; (g-i) SEM image and the elemental mappings of Au of CDs after adsorbing Au3+.
A schematic illustration of the synthesis of CDs and their application in Au3+ detection and reduction.
Detection of Au3+ in river water and tap water samples using CDs.

head.ce:title: Red fluorescent carbon dots for sensitive and selective detection and reduction of Au3+

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Simultaneous detection and reduction of Au3+ in contaminated water is extremely desirable. Herein, we synthesized red fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) for Au3+ detection with dual colorimetric and fluorescent spectral response within 2 min. The CDs showed high sensitivity and selectivity towards Au3+ with a low detection limit of 29 nM. Further, CDs selectively reduce Au3+ to Au0 via redox reaction between the thioether group on the surface of CDs and Au3+. Accordingly, CDs were effectively utilized to detect and extract Au3+ without interference from other metal ions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Gold (Au) is frequently employed in the field of science and technology owing to its extraordinary corrosion resistance and stability [1]. However, it can damage liver, kidneys, and nervous system in humans due to the strong coordination between Au3+ ions and enzymes [2,3]. Currently, analytical techniques including atomic absorption spectroscopy [4], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [5], electrochemistry [6], and other technologies are used to measure the levels of Au3+ [7]. However, they are time-consuming and require expert capabilities and sophisticated equipment [8], underscoring the need for advanced analytical techniques capable of monitoring and recovering trace amounts of Au3+ in aqueous environments [9–11].

Fluorescence-based detection has attracted increased attention owing to its low cost, ease of use, and high sensitivity and specificity [12–14]. Among the fluorescent materials available, carbon dots (CDs) are an excellent choice due to their ease of synthesis and surface modification, excellent biocompatibility, adjustable emission wavelength, outstanding photostability, and high fluorescence quantum yield [15–17]. The interaction between CDs and analyte induces changes in the fluorescent properties of the CDs, such as intensity, wavelength, and lifetime, which generate multiple signals for selective and sensitive monitoring of the analyte [18]. For example, Zhou et al. developed hydrophilic CDs (HCDs) utilizing methylene blue and sulfuric acid as carbon sources via solvothermal carbonization. The HCDs were employed to detect Au3+ by quenching the fluorescence of the CDs, which is generated by the inner filter effect (IFE) due to the interaction between HCDs and Au3+ [19]. Xu et al. utilized o-phenylenediamine and ethylene glycol as carbon sources to synthesize yellow-emitting carbon quantum dots (Y-CQDs) using a hydrothermal method. The addition of Au3+ quenches the fluorescence of Y-CQDs via synergistic fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and IFE mechanisms [20]. However, most published methods of fluorescence focus on a single objective, with few studies reporting the simultaneous determination and recovery of Au3+, as summarized in Table S1 [21–23].

Multifunctional materials integrated with detection and recycling capabilities are strongly in demand. Recently, Xu et al. synthesized melamine formaldehyde microspheres mixed with N and S co-doped CDs for simultaneous detection and absorption of Au3+ [24]. However, these microspheres exhibit good adsorption only under a strong acid pH (2) in aqueous solution, and the adsorption equilibrium can last up to 4 h. More importantly, this method can only adsorb Au3+ but cannot reduce Au3+ to Au0. Therefore, a sustainable and effective method is urgently needed to detect and reduce Au3+ in environmental water samples.

Here we synthesized red fluorescent CDs for the simultaneous, sensitive and selective detection and reduction of Au3+. As shown in Scheme 1, the CDs were prepared by hydrothermal treatment of neutral red and thiourea. The CDs exhibit red emission at a wavelength of 637 nm and excitation at 520 nm. The addition of Au3+ effectively quenches the fluorescence of CDs in phosphate buffer solution (PBS) within 2 min at pH 7 due to the coordination-induced aggregation. The CDs can be used to detect Au3+ in environmental water, including river water and tap water with satisfactory recoveries and low limit of detection (LOD). Impressively, CDs can reduce Au3+ to Au0 via redox reaction between thioether groups on the surface of CDs and Au3+, as verified by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and elemental mapping techniques.

Neutral red and thiourea were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). HAuCl4·XH2O was supplied by Macklin (Shanghai, China). The buffer solutions (pH range, 3–8) were prepared from NaH2PO4 and Na2HPO4 with different ratios. All purchased reagents are of analytical grade without further purification.

Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were observed using Nano-Scope (Bruker). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were acquired using the JEOL JEM-2100 F electron microscope. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were obtained with JSM-35CF. Raman spectrum was recorded using an Invia-Reflex Raman system. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra were obtained using an M15E-PS/10 (Bruker) IR spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using an Escalab 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo, USA). Absorption spectra were obtained using Shimadzu UV-2600 spectrophotometers, while fluorescence spectra were measured by Shimadzu RF-6000 spectrophotometers.

The CDs synthesis was started by dissolving 0.1 g neutral red and 0.1 g thiourea in 20 mL of ultrapure water, then transferred to a 50 mL Teflon autoclave. The mixture was heated at 180 ℃ for 4 h. After cooling the autoclave to room temperature, the resulting solution was centrifuged at 8500 rpm for 20 min to remove the precipitate. The upper clear solution was dialyzed with a dialysis bag having a molecular weight of 1000 Da. After 24 h of dialysis, the CDs solution was obtained. The aqueous solution of CDs was freeze-dried to obtain the powder.

Rhodamine B was selected as the standard, and the relative fluorescence quantum yield (FLQY) of the CDs (pH 7, 10 mM PBS) was calculated using the following equation:
φ

2
where Фref is the FLQY of Rhodamine B; η, I, and A represent the refractive index of the solvent, the integral area of fluorescence spectrum, and the absorbance at the excitation wavelength, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence determination of Au3+ in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical experiment, Au3+ was detected in PBS (10 mM, pH 7.0) at room temperature. CDs were dissolved in PBS at a pH of 7.0, followed by the addition of different concentrations of Au3+ solution. The fluorescence emission spectra in the range of 580–780 nm (λex = 520 nm) were collected after 2 min. The anti-interference ability of the CDs toward Au3+ was then investigated.

The red fluorescent CDs were synthesized hydrothermally utilizing neutral red and thiourea as the carbon sources and water as a solvent. The TEM image revealed a uniform distribution of CDs with an average diameter of 4.3 nm ( Figs. 1a and S1). High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image demonstrated a lattice spacing of 0.22 nm (Fig. 1a inset), which corresponds to the graphitic carbon (100) plane [25]. The height of CDs was ~4.2 nm based on AFM measurements (Fig. 1b). The Raman spectrum of CDs showed two graphene-like bands including the G-band and D-band peaks at 1563 cm−1 and 1349 cm−1, respectively. The intensity ratio (IG/ID) was 2.24, implying that CDs contain lattice defects and disordered carbon (Fig. 1c) [26]. Further, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectrum shown in Fig. S2 exhibited the characteristic graphitic carbon peak at 2θ = 25°, suggesting that the CDs have a graphite-like structure. The chemical functional groups of the CDs were studied using the FT-IR. As shown in Fig. 1d, the N-H exhibited a shorter vibrational absorption band at 3577 cm−1, whereas, O-H showed a broader vibrational absorption band at 3430 cm−1. The maxima of the CO/CN and CC vibration bands were 1637 and 1616 cm−1, respectively. Four peaks were observed at 1396 cm−1 due to C-N, 1354 cm−1 due to C-H, 1207 cm−1 due to C-S, and 1116 cm−1 attributed to C-O bonds [27]. Further, the zeta potential of CDs was + 3.89 mV, demonstrating the presence of -NH2 groups on the surface of CDs.

Further, the XPS was used to analyze the chemical composition and elemental distribution of CDs. As shown in Fig. S3a, five main peaks were detected in the survey spectrum of the CDs at 165.6, 198.9, 284.1, 400.0, and 533.1 eV, which were attributed to the S 2p, Cl 2p, C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s, respectively. The peaks at 337.8 and 356.1 eV originating from Pd 3d were attributed to the substrate [28]. The deconvolution of high-resolution C1s XPS spectra revealed four peaks at 284.4, 285.9, 287.3 and 288.1 eV corresponding to C-C, C-N/C-O, C-S, and CO, respectively (Fig. S3b). Three peaks were identified in the high-resolution N 1 s spectra, including peaks at 400.2, 399.5, and 398.7 eV, attributed to N-H, pyrrolic N, and pyridinic N, respectively (Fig. S3c). Additionally, the peaks of CO and O-H were detected at 532.3 and 531.0 eV, respectively, in the O 1 s high-resolution spectra shown in Fig. S3d. The three peaks of S 2p at 167.6, 164.6, and 163.4 eV were attributed to -SO4 2-, S 2p1/2, and S 2p3/2, respectively (Fig. S3e) [29]. The XPS results were consistent with the FT-IR results, indicating the presence of a substantial number of -OH, -COOH, -NH2, and S-containing chemical groups on the surface of CDs.

The UV-Vis absorption spectrum of the prepared CDs exhibited broad absorption in the wavelength ranges of 275–600 nm ( Fig. 2a). The absorption peak at 275 nm was ascribed to the π-π * transition of CC, whereas the absorption peak at 380–600 nm was caused by the n-π * transition of CO, demonstrating the presence of CDs in a larger-sized conjugated sp2 structure [30]. Meanwhile, Fig. 2a revealed that the fluorescence intensity was maximum at 637 nm, independent of excitation wavelength (Fig. 2b), indicating that CDs have a uniform surface nanostructure [31]. The fluorescence quantum yield of CDs was measured to be 13 % by using Rhodamine B as the standard. The photostability and anti-photobleaching properties of CDs were measured and compared with sodium fluorescein. As illustrated in Figs. 2c, d, and S4, the absorbance of CDs at 468 nm generally remained unchanged after 60 min of irradiation using a 1 W/cm2 xenon lamp, while the absorbance of fluorescein sodium at 480 nm was significantly reduced. Further, the anti-photobleaching ability of CDs was also better than that of sodium fluorescein (Fig. 2d). These results confirmed the excellent photostability and anti-photobleaching ability of CDs, which were the two prerequisites for an advanced optical sensing material. The CDs aqueous solution also exhibited good temporal stability. As shown in Fig. S5, the fluorescence spectra showed no obvious changes within 7 days.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimizing conditions and fluorescent detection of Au3+
ce:section.ce:para: The pH has great effect on the optical properties of CDs. As shown in Fig. S6, the absorbance at 529 nm decreased and blue-shifted with increasing pH, the solution color changed from pink to yellow. At pH 3, the CDs dissolved in the solution exhibited the highest fluorescent intensity, but the fluorescence intensity decreased significantly as the pH increased to 7 and 8 ( Fig. 3a). Under acidic conditions, the CDs contained a high number of positive charges and resulted in electrostatic repulsion among CDs, resulting in enhanced fluorescence. In contrast, under neutral and alkaline conditions, the fluorescence was quenched due to electrostatic attraction, which induced CDs aggregation [32].

In practical applications, response time is another critical indicator of fluorescent probes. The fluorescence intensity of CDs was decreased by the addition of 6 μM Au3+, as shown in Fig. 3b. The reaction was completed quickly in PBS within 2 min at pH 7, which was faster than at pH 5 or 6. Therefore, the following experiments were been performed at pH 7 in PBS. The spectral response of CDs to Au3+ in PBS at pH 7 was further investigated. As shown in Fig. 3c, the absorbance of CDs at 380–600 nm was decreased after Au3+ addition, while the absorbance at 275 nm was increased. Correspondingly, the color of the aqueous solution of CDs changed from red to colorless (Fig. 3c, inset). Therefore, CDs can be used for colorimetric detection of Au3+. Meanwhile, the fluorescence of CDs was gradually quenched by adding Au3+ in the range of 1.0 μM to 8.0 μM (Fig. 3d). The fluorescence intensity of CDs at 637 nm showed excellent linear relationship when the concentration of Au3+ was in the range of 1.0 μM to 5.0 μM (Fig. S7). The detection limit of Au3+ using the CDs was as low as 29 nM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity of CDs toward Au3+ in analysis of real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of other metal ions and organic pollutants on the fluorescence of CDs was analyzed to determine the specificity of CDs to Au3+. As shown in Figs. 4 and S8a, the inclusion of other metal ions and organic pollutants did not affect the fluorescence of CDs. However, when 8 μM Au3+ was added, the fluorescence intensity of CDs was significantly decreased even in the presence of these ions, indicating that CDs exhibit excellent specificity for Au3+. In addition, the real water sample containing 100 μM of other metal ions was added to the CDs solution [33]. As shown in Fig. S8b, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs solution remained stable even in the presence of the high concentration of other metal ions, but was quenched significantly after the addition of Au3+ (8 μM). These results indicate the good anti-interference ability of the CDs. The most plausible explanation is that Au3+ coordinates with the N and O containing groups on the surface of CDs, inducing CDs aggregation to quench fluorescence.

To investigate the sensing viability of CDs in environmental water samples, the CDs were used to detect Au3+ concentrations of 2, 4, and 6 μM in river and tap water samples lacking Au3+, as mentioned in Table 1. The acceptable recovery rate ranged between 96 % and 104 %, with a relative standard deviation of less than 3.9 %. These results demonstrate that the CDs were highly capable of detecting Au3+ in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The sensing mechanism of CDs and reduction of Au3+ in wastewater
ce:section.ce:para: To further verify the quenching mechanism, the wavelengths of the two isosbestic points of 423 and 597 nm were utilized as excitation wavelengths, and the response to Au3+ was measured. As demonstrated in Fig. S9, the fluorescence was quenched as the Au3+ concentration increased, indicating that the quenching fluorescence of the CDs was not caused by the decrease in the absorbed light. The aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching of CDs by Au3+ was demonstrated via TEM and AFM. As shown in Fig. 5a and b, many large particles were observed after the addition of Au3+. To elucidate the quenching mechanism, the fluorescence lifetimes of CDs were determined before and after the addition of Au3+. The results showed a significant decrease in fluorescence lifetime from 7.57 ns to 6.61 ns after the addition of Au3+ (Fig. 5c). These results revealed that the CDs fluorescence quenched by Au3+ originated via a dynamic quenching mechanism.

In the case of Au3+ detection and recovery, most of the materials tend to be focused on a single aspect. However, from an economic and energy-saving perspective, dual-functional materials offer significant benefits for both Au3+ detection and reduction. Further, CDs can reduce Au3+ to Au, based on our study results. Addition of 100 μM Au3+ to CDs (9.6 μg/mL) resulted in visible precipitation, while no change was detected upon the addition of other metal ions (Fig. S10).

To further investigate the formation of Au based on the reduction mechanism, the FT-IR spectrum of CDs-Au3+ mixture was measured. The results revealed that the intensity of C-S groups were increased by the addition of Au3+, indicating that the reduction might be caused by the nucleophilic sulfur-containing group and Au3+ (Fig. 5d). XPS was also utilized to analyze the chemical composition of Au3+ after adding CDs. The resolved Au 4 f peaks at 84.4, 86.8, 88.3, and 90.3 eV corresponded to Au04 f7/2, Au3+4 f7/2, Au04 f5/2, and Au3+4 f5/2, respectively (Fig. 5e and f) [34]. The appearance of Au0 validated the Au3+ reduction by CDs, which was further verified by SEM-EDS. As shown in Fig. 5g–i, the Au mapping results of EDS showed that a large number of Au particles were adsorbed on the surface of the carbon material. The atomic percentages of Au and C elements were calculated to be 45 % and 33.5 %, respectively.

In summary, we employed neutral red and thiourea as carbon sources for hydrothermal synthesis of CDs. The CDs with a red fluorescence peaked at 637 nm. The fluorescence of CDs was selectively and sensitively quenched by Au3+ at pH 7, with a response time of less than 2 min and a detection limit of 29 nM. The recovery efficiency and relative standard deviation of Au3+ detection using CDs in tap water and river water samples are satisfactory. Further, the results showed that CDs can successfully recycle Au0 by reducing Au3+. The mechanism demonstrates that the redox reaction and coordination between sulfide groups on the surface of CDs with Au3+ primarily contributed to fluorescence quenching and recovery. The method for detecting Au3+ is simple, convenient, low-cost, and effective, with potential application in the development of new biological and environmental sensors.

Minhuan Lan planed and supervised the project. Xiangcao Li and Minhuan Lan designed and carried out the detailed experiments, analyzed the data and wrote the whole paper. Qian Hu and Ke Yang helped for the fluorescence and absorption determination for Au3+. Shaojing Zhao, Shaohua Zhu, and Benhua Wang contributed to the structure characterization and photophysical experiments of CDs. Yi Zhang, Jianing Yi, and Xiangzhi Song contributed to the analysis of the data, mechanism explanation, and discussion of the paper.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 62175262), Fundamental Research Fund for the Central South University (No. 2020CX021), and Key R & D plan of Hunan Province (No. 2022SK2101).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.132534.

ce:section.ce:para:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multifunctional fluorescent “Off-On-Off” nanosensor for Au3+ and S2- employing N-S co-doped carbonedots
Carbon

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: V. Sharma, N. Kaur, P. Tiwari, A.K. Saini, S.M. Mobin, Multifunctional fluorescent “Off-On-Off” nanosensor for Au3+ and S2- employing N-S co-doped carbonedots. Carbon 139 (2018) 393–403.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Xiangcao Li received her B.S. degree from Anhui University of Technology in 2019. She entered the MS course in Central South University in 2019, majored in organic chemistry. Now, she is engaged in the development and application of nanomaterials and fluorescent probes.

Qian Hu received her B.S. degree from Central South University in 2021. She entered the MS course in Central South University in 2021, majored in chemical engineering. Her current research interests focus on the synthesis and applications of nanomaterials and functional polymers.

Ke Yang received her B.S. degree from Wuhan Institute of Technology in 2019. She entered the MS course in Central South University in 2019, majored in organic chemistry. Currently, she is concentrated in the application of nanomaterials.

Shaojing Zhao received her Ph.D. degree from Tianjing University. Then, she became a post-doctoral researcher at the City University of Hong Kong (from 2016 to 2017). She joined Central South University (CSU) in 2017. Her research mainly engaged in the research of surfactants and photo-functional materials.
Shaohua Zhu received her B.S. degree from Hunan Institute of Technology, majoring in computer science. Currently, he is mainly engaged in the detection and removal of pollutants in water.

Benhua Wang is an associate professor at College of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Central South University. She received her PhD degree from Heidelberg University in 2018. Her current research interests focus on developing novel fluorescent probes and constructing fluorescent sensor arrays.

Yi zhang got a PhD degree from Central South University. Currently, he is a Professor at the Third Xiangya Hospital of Central South University, and His current research interests focus on the developing optical materials to investigate the pathogenesis of colorectal cancer disease.

Jianing Yi received his B.S. degree from Hunan Normal University in 2017, He entered the MS course in Hunan Normal University in 2018, majored in medicine and received MS degree in 2021, then he joined Surgical Department of Breast and Thyroid Gland, Hunan Provincial People’s Hospital, The Affiliated First Hospital of Hunan Normal University in 2021. Now, he is engaged in the development and application of nanomaterials in cancer.

Xiangzhi Song is a professor at College of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Central South University. He got a PhD degree at College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Peking University. His current research interests focus on the design and synthesis of red-emitting fluorescent dyes with large Stokes shift, NIR fluorophores and organelle targetable fluorescent probes.

Minhuan Lan received his Ph.D. degree in organic chemistry in 2013 from the Technical Institute of Physics and Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences (TIPC, CAS) under the supervision of Prof. Pengfei Wang and Prof. Wenjun Zhang. Then, he joined Prof. Wenjun Zhang’s group as a post-doctoral researcher at the City University of Hong Kong (from 2013 to 2017). He joined Central South University (CSU) in 2017. Now, he is a Professor at CSU, and his current research interests include design and development of fluorescent materials and investigation their applications in biosensing, bioimaging, and phototherapy.
